why_limit_results.label: Why limit results
why_limit_results.summary: Learn why LIMIT is useful for showing only the most relevant rows after sorting, especially when you want quick, readable answers.

sketch1.title: LIMIT works on the final ordered rows

sketch2.title: Use LIMIT to focus on the answer you need

ci_lowest_stock_2.title: Return the 2 products with the lowest stock

sc-limit-purpose.title: What LIMIT does
sc-limit-purpose.prompt: What does `LIMIT 3` do in a SQL query?
sc-limit-purpose.hint: Think about how many rows the results table should show.

help.concept: `LIMIT` controls the maximum number of rows returned in the result set.
help.hint_1: It does not sort rows or choose columns. It only restricts how many rows appear.
help.hint_2: If a query would normally return 6 rows, adding `LIMIT 3` cuts that down to only the first 3 rows of the final result.

sc-order-then-limit.title: Sort before limiting
sc-order-then-limit.prompt: In `SELECT name, price FROM products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 2;`, which rows are returned?
sc-order-then-limit.hint: Look at the sort direction before thinking about the row count.

help.concept: When `ORDER BY` and `LIMIT` are used together, SQL first orders the rows, then keeps only the first rows from that ordered result.
help.hint_2: After sorting from highest price to lowest price, `LIMIT 2` keeps the first two products in that list.

options.d: All products except the 2 most expensive

mc-good-limit-uses.title: Good uses for LIMIT
mc-good-limit-uses.prompt: Which tasks are good reasons to use `LIMIT`? Choose all that apply.
mc-good-limit-uses.hint: Think about cases where you want only a small part of the full result.

help.concept: `LIMIT` is useful when you want a shorter result set, especially for top, bottom, newest, or preview-style queries.
help.hint_1: Look for tasks like showing only the first few rows after sorting.
help.hint_2: Tasks that ask for the top 3, cheapest 1, or a quick preview fit `LIMIT`; tasks about renaming columns or filtering by condition do not require it.

options.b: Preview only the first 2 rows of a sorted result
options.d: Find just the cheapest product after sorting by price

mc-limit-benefits.title: Why limiting results is helpful
mc-limit-benefits.prompt: Why can `LIMIT` make query results easier to use? Choose all that apply.
mc-limit-benefits.hint: Think about readability and focus in the results table.

help.concept: `LIMIT` helps reduce clutter and focus attention on the most relevant rows in the result set.
help.hint_1: Smaller result sets are easier to scan when you only need a few rows.
help.hint_2: Choose the ideas about readability and showing only the most relevant rows, not ideas about changing table data.

options.a: It can make the results table shorter and easier to read
options.b: It helps focus on the most relevant rows after sorting
options.c: It permanently deletes extra rows from the table
options.d: It can help when you only need a quick sample of rows

dr-top-price-sequence.title: Build a top-priced query
dr-top-price-sequence.prompt: Put these SQL parts in the correct order to return the 2 most expensive products.
dr-top-price-sequence.hint: Start with selecting columns, then the table, then sorting, then limiting.

help.concept: A basic query follows the clause order `SELECT`, `FROM`, `ORDER BY`, then `LIMIT`.
help.hint_1: The sort must happen before the row limit so SQL knows which rows come first.
help.hint_2: Use descending price order, then keep only 2 rows.

dr-cheapest-one-sequence.title: Build a cheapest-product query
dr-cheapest-one-sequence.prompt: Arrange the SQL parts to return the single cheapest product.
dr-cheapest-one-sequence.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fb-limit-number.title: Complete a LIMIT clause
fb-limit-number.prompt: Fill in the missing value to return only 2 rows.
fb-limit-number.hint: The blank should be the number of rows you want back.

help.concept: The number after `LIMIT` sets the maximum row count in the result.
help.hint_1: If you want exactly two rows shown, the clause needs that row count.
help.hint_2: Choose the numeric value that matches the requested number of rows.

fb-sort-direction.title: Complete the sort for highest prices
fb-sort-direction.prompt: Fill in the missing keyword so the highest prices appear first.
fb-sort-direction.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

fb-sort-direction.template: SELECT name, price FROM products ORDER BY price [blank1] LIMIT 3;

sketch-why-limit-matters.title: Why LIMIT helps
sketch-why-limit-matters.bodyMarkdown:
When a table has many rows, showing everything can make the answer hard to read. `LIMIT` tells SQL to return only a small number of rows.

A common pattern is to sort first, then limit the result.

```sql
SELECT name, price
FROM products
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 3;
```

This query means:
- `SELECT name, price` shows only the `name` and `price` columns.
- `FROM products` reads from the `products` table.
- `ORDER BY price DESC` puts the most expensive products first.
- `LIMIT 3` keeps only the first 3 rows after sorting.

With the dataset in this lesson, the result would be the 3 highest-priced products:
- Office Chair
- Desk Lamp
- Acrylic Paint Set

Without `LIMIT`, you would still get the sorted list, but you would see all products.

sketch-limit-after-sorting.title: LIMIT works on the final ordered rows
sketch-limit-after-sorting.bodyMarkdown:
A key idea is that `LIMIT` applies after SQL has arranged the rows for the result.

Compare these two goals:

```sql
SELECT name, created_at
FROM products
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 2;
```

This finds the 2 most recently created products.

Step by step:
- `ORDER BY created_at DESC` puts newer dates first.
- `LIMIT 2` keeps only the first 2 rows from that ordered list.

In this dataset, those rows are:
- Notebook (`2026-02-14`)
- Acrylic Paint Set (`2026-02-02`)

You can use the same idea with other columns, such as `stock` or `price`, depending on what you want to inspect.

sketch-limit-for-focus.title: Use LIMIT to focus on the answer you need
sketch-limit-for-focus.bodyMarkdown:
`LIMIT` is useful when you want a short, focused answer instead of a full table.

For example, if you only want the cheapest product:

```sql
SELECT name, price
FROM products
ORDER BY price ASC
LIMIT 1;
```

Line by line:
- `ORDER BY price ASC` puts the lowest price first.
- `LIMIT 1` returns just that first row.

In this dataset, that row is `Notebook` with a price of `6.75`.

This is helpful for questions like:
- What is the cheapest item?
- Which 3 products have the highest stock?
- What are the 2 newest products?
